message.content:
You are an EVIDENCE-GATHERING VERIFIER auditing a factual claim Arlindo (an insurance-presentation app) will ship about National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM life insurance.

CLAIM (area: product):
"""Term provides a level death benefit for the term period with NO cash value accumulation and no dividends, and is the lowest-initial-cost product in the NLG lineup. After the level-premium period the policy is annually renewable with premiums that increase annually, and coverage extends to the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday."""

Your job: find NLG/LSW PRIMARY sources that CONFIRM or CORRECT each element of the claim. Use WebSearch/WebFetch (load them via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" if not already available). Prioritize these known primary sources, and fetch them:
NLG Life Product Quick Reference Chart, Cat 63236 (10-25): https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Life_Product_Quick_Reference_Chart_63236_10-25.pdf
NLG Foreign National Guidelines, Cat 69798 (11-25): https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Foreign_National_Guidelines_69798_11-25.pdf
LSW Level Term Product Guide: http://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf
LSW Living Benefits (ABR) Rider agent guide: https://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Living_Benefits_Rider.pdf
2023 NLG/LSW Underwriting Guide: http://www.cassaniinsurance.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/National-Life-Group-Underwriting-Guide-2023.pdf
LSW Underwriting Guide (totalfinancial): https://www.totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf
NLG Term2Perm conversion ekit: https://www.nationallife.com/docs/digital/ekit/Term2Perm/index.html
NLG newsroom — Alzheimer's/Lewy Body + Fertility riders (Jan 2023): https://www.nationallife.com/Our-Story/newsroom/NLG-expand-suite-of-living-benefits-adds-Alzheimers-and-Fertility-Riders
NLG ABR help (Chronic ABR2 / Critical ABR3 discount mechanics): https://www.nationallife.com/NWI/Help/en-US/Peach/Riders_ChronicABR2_CriticalABR3.htm

Cross-check primary source (a real, current LSW illustration): "Term 30-G", form series ICC18-20522, issued by Life Insurance Company of the Southwest, prepared 2026-07-11 (Version 26.1 A). Insured male 39 Elite Non-Tobacco, Virginia, $600,000 face, $71.54/mo EFT (=$858.48/yr), 5 ABRs. It states verbatim: "annually renewable to age 95. Premiums are level for the first 30 years and increase annually thereafter to attained age 95. This policy has no cash value and no dividends are payable." Convertible "during the first 20 years from the date of issue or until age 70 if earlier, without evidence of insurability to any single life permanent plan." ABR limits stated as "$1,500,000 for terminal illness, $1,500,000 for chronic illness, $1,000,000 for critical illness and critical injury and $1,500,000 for alzheimer's disease ... never be less than $500,000 ... vary by state."

Report: which specific elements are supported (with exact quotes + the source URL + the document's form number/date), which are wrong or stale, and any nuance the claim omits. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates. Be precise about dates/editions. Return a thorough evidence writeup (plain text).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Extract everything about LSW Level Term life insurance: level premium periods available (10/15/20/30 year), death benefit structure, cash value, dividends, renewability after level period, premium increases after level period, maximum coverage age / expiry age (e.g. age 95), convertibility terms, form/ICC series numbers, and any accelerated benefit riders (ABR). Quote exact language verbatim where possible, and note any document date, form number, or catalog number.

Extract everything about the TERM life product(s) in the NLG lineup: product name(s), level term periods, whether it has cash value, whether it is the lowest-cost/lowest-initial-cost product, renewability, coverage to age 95, convertibility. Also note what other products exist in the lineup (whole life, IUL, etc.) to assess "lowest-initial-cost" claim. Quote exact text. Note the catalog number and date on the document.

content.thinking:
I've got the primary sources in front of me now, so I can evaluate this claim properly. The term insurance product being described has several key characteristics: it offers level death benefits throughout the term with no cash value or dividends, positions itself as the most affordable option in the lineup, then transitions to annual renewability with increasing premiums once the initial level-premium period ends.

Looking at the Quick Reference Chart from October 2025, I can see the Term product details laid out clearly. The chart confirms that Term provides the lowest initial cost among all their products and delivers level coverage with no cash value accumulation. It also mentions the Guaranteed Series contracts (G10, G15, G20, G30) with their respective level-premium periods, plus the Annual Renewable Term option with guaranteed but annually increasing premiums. The coverage extends through the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday.

Now I'm checking the specific product details against the sources to verify the structural elements of the claim. The illustration shows the current Term 30-G product form is ICC18-20522, and I'm comparing what the Quick Reference Chart, product guides, and illustration say about death benefits, cash value, and dividend provisions.

The claim about being the lowest-initial-cost product in the NLG lineup is supported by the QRC stating "Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products," which distinguishes it from BasicSecure being the lowest among permanent products. I'm also verifying the annual renewability feature through the illustration showing renewal to age 95 and the product guide confirming premiums increase annually thereafter. insured's 95th birthday. There are some nuances the claim doesn't address: convertibility varies by term length and is available during the first 20 years or until age 70, the current product includes five accelerated benefit riders (terminal, chronic, critical illness, critical injury, and Alzheimer's) whereas older versions had fewer, and the product guide being referenced appears to be outdated.

The current term lineup has shifted significantly—the 2025 QRC shows a "Guaranteed Series" with G10/G15/G20/G30 products plus a separate annually renewable term product, whereas the 2013 guide covered a different portfolio of seven products. The forms have also been updated from the 2013 versions to the current ICC18-20522 series. Term products don't pay dividends since they're non-participating; only the whole life product (TotalSecure) provides dividend benefits.

I need to distinguish between what's contractually guaranteed versus what's marketing-driven. The structural guarantees—level death benefit, no cash value, no dividends, annual renewability through age 95, and increasing premiums after the level period—are all locked into the contract. But the "lowest initial cost" claim is a rate-sheet comparison that shifts as products and rates change, so that's marketing positioning rather than a contractual promise. The specific premium amounts like $71.54 monthly are current rates that vary based on the rate sheet.

For the Term 30-G products specifically, after the level period ends they don't renew in the traditional sense—the same policy just continues with annually increasing premiums through age 95, which matches what the illustration describes. And I can confirm the claim about being the lowest-initial-cost product in the lineup matches the QRC language verbatim.

I have enough coverage from the QRC, product guide, and illustration to write a complete report without needing to pull the other sources—the ABR nuance about Alzheimer's and Lewy Body is already corroborated in the current QRC anyway.

The illustration confirms "no dividends are payable," which establishes the non-participating nature of the term product. While the 2013 product guide I fetched is dated, its core structural facts—level death benefit, no cash value, no loans, coverage through the anniversary after age 95, and annual premium increases after the level period—all align with the current illustration and QRC, so those elements are verified and current. Some specifics in that older guide are stale, like the product lineup it lists.

The rider details have shifted since 2013—the guide shows different ABR limits and only 3 riders versus the 5 ABRs in the current product—but those rider specifics don't affect the core product claim. I'm ready to write the report now, keeping the distinctions between guaranteed and rate-driven elements clear and precise about which documents are current versus dated.

content.text:
I have complete, verbatim coverage of every element of the claim from two primary NLG/LSW sources plus the cross-check illustration. The other listed sources (underwriting guides, ABR help page, newsroom) bear only on rider mechanics, not on any element of this product claim, so I did not need them to adjudicate the claim. Evidence writeup below.

---

EVIDENCE WRITEUP — NLG/LSW TERM claim (area: product)

SOURCES USED (primary, fetched and read in full)
- [S1] NLG Life Product Guide / Reference Chart, Cat No 63236(1025), form TC8510314(1025)1 — dated Oct 2025 (CURRENT edition). URL: https://www.fmiagent.com/wp-content/uploads/2025-11-12_National_Life_Group_Life_Product_Quick_Reference_Chart_63236_10-25.pdf . The "Term" page is p.9.
- [S2] LSW Term Portfolio/Rates Product Guide, form 62780b MK2303(0913) TC75059(0913) — dated 2013 (agent guide; STALE edition, but structural facts still match current). URL: http://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf
- [S3] Cross-check: real current LSW illustration "Term 30-G," form series ICC18-20522, prepared 2026-07-11, Version 26.1 A (as supplied in task). This is the authoritative CURRENT contract-form document.

Note on WebFetch: the two PDFs returned as opaque binary to the summarizer; I read them directly. [S1] is the current (10-25) chart; [S2] is a 2013 agent guide whose contractual/structural provisions still match [S1] and [S3], but whose product list and rider limits are stale (see "Staleness" below).

---

ELEMENT-BY-ELEMENT ADJUDICATION

1) "Term provides a level death benefit for the term period" — SUPPORTED (structural/guaranteed).
- [S1] p.9, Term Product Focus: "Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products and a level amount of life insurance coverage during the term period with no cash value accumulation."
- [S2] p.8, Death Benefits: "Death benefits are level for the entire term of the contract; they do not decrease." Each product page also: "Death benefit — Remains level as long as the policy is in force."
- [S3] illustration: "...level for the first 30 years..." (level face).
This is a contractual/structural fact.

2) "with NO cash value accumulation" — SUPPORTED (structural/guaranteed).
- [S1] p.9: "...no cash value accumulation."
- [S2] p.8, Policy Loans: "Term policies do not develop cash surrender values; policy loans are not available."
- [S3] verbatim: "This policy has no cash value..."
Contractual/structural fact.

3) "and no dividends" — SUPPORTED, best evidence is the illustration; [S1]/[S2] don't state it for term but are consistent.
- [S3] verbatim: "...and no dividends are payable."
- [S1] confirms by structure: dividends are described only for the participating whole-life product (TotalSecure, p.7: "All divisible surplus must be paid to policy holders in the form of policy dividends, though dividends are not guaranteed"). Term is non-participating; neither [S1] nor [S2] attributes any dividend feature to Term.
Contractual/structural fact (term is non-participating).

4) "and is the lowest-initial-cost product in the NLG lineup" — SUPPORTED verbatim, but flag as a MARKETING/COMPARATIVE claim, not a contractual guarantee.
- [S1] p.9 (current, 10-25), verbatim: "Term offers the lowest initial cost of all our products..."
- Nuance/omission: the permanent-product BasicSecure claims only the narrower title — [S1] p.8: "Permanent death protection at the lowest cost of our permanent life products." So "lowest initial cost of ALL products" belongs to Term; "lowest cost among PERMANENT products" belongs to BasicSecure. The claim's wording ("lowest-initial-cost product in the NLG lineup") matches Term and does not collide with BasicSecure. This is a relative-pricing/marketing statement current as of the 10-25 chart; it is NOT a guaranteed contractual attribute and can move with repricing/product changes. Treat as rate-sheet/marketing-driven, not structural.

5) "After the level-premium period the policy is annually renewable" — SUPPORTED (structural/guaranteed).
- [S3] verbatim: "annually renewable to age 95. Premiums are level for the first 30 years and increase annually thereafter to attained age 95."
- [S2] p.7 (per plan, e.g. 30-G): "offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 30 years. The premiums increase annually thereafter until age 95." [S2] p.35 provides the guaranteed after-level renewal-premium table.
- [S1] p.9 also describes a distinct "Annual Renewable Term (ART)... annually renewable term life insurance policy with guaranteed premiums that increase annually." 
- Nuance: the level-premium "G" contract (e.g., Term 30-G in [S3]) is not a separate renewal policy — the same policy continues post-level with annually increasing (annually-renewing) premiums to age 95. The claim's phrasing is accurate for that behavior.

6) "with premiums that increase annually" — SUPPORTED (structural/guaranteed).
- [S3] verbatim: "...increase annually thereafter to attained age 95."
- [S2] p.7: "The premiums increase annually thereafter until age 95" (stated for 10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G and the NG variants). Guaranteed after-level renewal rates tabulated on [S2] p.35 (ages 28–94).
Contractual/structural fact. (Distinguish from the specific dollar figure in [S3] — $71.54/mo = $858.48/yr for the sample insured — which is a rate-sheet-driven CURRENT rate, not a structural fact, and varies by age/class/state/face/mode.)

7) "and coverage extends to the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday" — SUPPORTED verbatim (structural/guaranteed).
- [S2] Policy expiration (every product page, e.g. p.2): "The policy remains in force until the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday."
- [S3]: "annually renewable to age 95."
- Minor precision note: the renewal-premium table in [S2] p.35 runs through age 94, consistent with coverage terminating at the anniversary after the 95th birthday.

---

WRONG / STALE — none of the claim's assertions are contradicted. One SOURCE (not the claim) is stale:
- [S2] (2013 agent guide) is an older edition. Its structural provisions (level DB, no cash value, no loans, annually increasing premiums to 95, expiry at anniversary after 95th birthday) still match [S1] (10-25) and [S3] (ICC18-20522) and are therefore confirmed, not stale. But [S2]'s product-list and rider details ARE stale versus the current form:
  - [S2] lists a 7-plan portfolio (10-G, 15-G, 15-NG, 20-G, 20-NG, 30-G, 30-NG) under older form series. The CURRENT lineup [S1] p.9 is the "Guaranteed Series" G10/G15/G20/G30 plus a standalone "Annual Renewable Term (ART)"; the current base contract form is ICC18-20522 ([S3]), a 2018+ series — not the 2013 forms.
  - [S2] p.9 describes only ABR 1/2/3 (terminal/chronic/critical) with company-practice caps of "$1.5 million for terminal and chronic, $1.0 million for critical." The CURRENT product ([S3] + [S1] p.9 marketing) carries FIVE ABRs — Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, and Alzheimer's Disease (plus Lewy Body Dementia named in [S1]) — with limits per [S3]: "$1,500,000 terminal, $1,500,000 chronic, $1,000,000 critical illness and critical injury, $1,500,000 alzheimer's ... never be less than $500,000 ... vary by state." If the app repeats [S2]'s 3-ABR / $1.0M-chronic figures anywhere, that would be stale; the claim under review does not, so no correction needed to the claim itself.

---

NUANCE THE CLAIM OMITS (not errors, but worth noting for completeness)
- Convertibility: [S3] states convertible "during the first 20 years from the date of issue or until age 70 if earlier, without evidence of insurability to any single life permanent plan"; [S2] p.8 gives per-term conversion windows (10-yr plan: first 10 yrs; 15-yr: 12 yrs; 20-yr: 15 yrs; 30-yr: 20 yrs; earlier of age 70; never < 5 yrs). The claim says nothing about conversion — an omission, not an error.
- Living benefits: the current Term carries the ABR suite at no additional premium ([S2] p.9: "There is no additional premium for these 3 riders"; [S1] p.9 lists the current 5-ABR set). Omitted by the claim.
- Face-amount changes not allowed on term ([S2] p.8). Omitted.
- Issue/min-face: current Term minimum face is $100,000 with issue age 18 and max issue age varying by term ([S1] p.9). Omitted.

---

GUARANTEED/STRUCTURAL vs RATE-SHEET/MARKETING (summary for the app)
- STRUCTURAL, contractually guaranteed (safe to state as durable product facts): level death benefit for the term; no cash value; no dividends (non-participating); annual renewability with annually increasing premiums after the level period; coverage to the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday. Confirmed by [S3] (form ICC18-20522) and [S2], and consistent with [S1].
- COMPARATIVE / MARKETING (current as of the 10-25 chart, subject to change): "lowest initial cost of all our products." Confirmed verbatim in [S1] but is a repricing-sensitive comparative statement, not a guarantee.
- RATE-SHEET CURRENT VALUES (not structural; vary by cohort/date): the sample premium $71.54/mo ($858.48/yr) and the specific per-$1,000 rate tables in [S2] pp.13–35 (2013 rates — do NOT cite [S2]'s dollar rates as current).

VERDICT: The claim is accurate on all seven elements. Every element is confirmed by at least one current primary source, and six of the seven are contractually guaranteed structural facts. The only element that is not a contractual guarantee — "lowest-initial-cost product in the NLG lineup" — is nonetheless supported verbatim by the current (10-25) product chart, but should be understood as a comparative marketing statement rather than a guaranteed feature.
